turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The initial goal of this appeal was to reverse a single Board of Immigration Appeals decision that changed the removability analysis for drug paraphernalia convictions. It abandoned the longstanding practice that Congress had ratified. We wanted to reinstate the rule that still applies in all other drug deportation decisions which looks at the facts that must be established by a conviction and at the record of conviction, when that's appropriate, under this Court's precedents in Moncrieffe and Descamps. That methodology has led to tens of thousands of drug deportations each year, but not for someone convicted of possessing a sock. Now, the government has argued that this wrongheaded BIA paraphernalia decision should be expanded to all drug convictions. The government wants any drug conviction to be a deportable offense, even if it's clearly for a non簫Federal drug. This Court should reject the government's position and the BIA's position and adhere to the time簫tested approach that the statute 簫簫 to the 簫簫 to the statute that Congress has ratified.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Can I 簫簫

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Can we begin by looking at the text of the statute? What does the phrase "relating to a controlled substance," et cetera, modify?

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Relating to serves the function of connecting the very broad range of offenses that Congress wants to make deportable to the list of substances that 簫簫 that the 簫簫 that Congress has specified.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, let's say we were 簫簫 let's say we're back in high school and we have to diagram this sentence.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: We could start out that way, and maybe we'll get beyond it, but let's start out that way. We have a phrase here, "relating to a controlled substance." What does it modify? It has to modify a noun. What noun does it modify?

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: "Relating to" would modify 簫簫 would modify "law" or "regulation." But if you bought 簫簫 if you diagram the sentence, everything 簫簫

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: If it modifies "law," then I think you've lost the case.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: But if 簫簫 if you diagram the sentence, though, everything in that whole phrase, "convicted of a violation of a law or regulation relating to a controlled substance under Section 802," all modifies "alien." The whole phrase has to be taken together and read together to modify what alien is deportable. And 簫簫 and the pure grammar does 簫簫 is not sufficient to determine what Congress meant when it enacted that text.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: If you 簫簫 if you 簫簫 I think Justice Alito is right. If you're claiming that, then you're accepting that there's ambiguity, and you have an uphill battle to fight.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Well, again, given the text and the context of this statute and the history of its application over decades and decades, um there's 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: That's an argument that they're arbitrary and capricious.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Well, it 簫簫 it's also part of the analysis that goes into determining the Chevron, the question that you allude to, Justice Sotomayor, because Chevron should be approached after applying the usual principles of statutory interpretation, and that's 簫簫 that's what Chevron says.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Wait, wait. How does it 簫簫 how does it 簫簫 I've missed this. I 簫簫 I'm missing something. Why 簫簫 why do you lose the case?

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Well 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I mean, I 簫簫 I assume that 簫簫 I assumed as you said 簫簫 what 簫簫 what are you fighting here? I assumed, as you said, that 簫簫 that the words 簫簫

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, that was my question.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: 簫簫 relating to a controlled substance as defined in Section 102 concern any law or regulation. And 簫簫

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: And that's only 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: You deny that?

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Well 簫簫 and 簫簫 but that's only part of the analysis.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I understand that it's not. I thought that was not your argument to deny that. Your argument was that this 簫簫 where 簫簫 where you have a 簫簫 a State law or a foreign law or something, and it does relate to the regulation of a controlled substance, but a lot of other things as well, that that falls outside.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Yes. And particularly in a sentence that 簫簫 that has such a focus on the word "convicted."

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Suppose it relates to the State law, there 簫簫 there are 36 802 substances and here it has 簫簫 it relates to 37. That's case one. Case two, it relates to 4,037.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: And both of these statutes contain the 36 that are in Section 802. One contains 4,000 more, and one contains one more. How am I supposed to analyze those two statutes?

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: This goes to the application of the categorical approach and the modified categorical approach. If the identity of the substance is 簫簫 is an element of the offense, then you use the modified categorical approach, you look at the record of conviction, and you determine what substance is involved. And if it's a substance on the 802 list, then the person is deportable. And as the analysis that we've provided in the appendix to our reply brief shows, many, many, many State statutes um are divisible in exactly that manner.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Okay. So your 簫簫 your argument is, it relates to 4,036, and 36 are 802s. And so what we do is we read the statute. It doesn't tell us what this conviction is about. We look to the documents in the modified categorical. If it lists one of the 36, that's the end of it, good簫bye, he's deported.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: If it doesn't, we don't know. If we don't know, he wins.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Yes. That's exactly right.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, that 簫簫 that might be sensible, but I 簫簫 I really do need to start with the text of the statute and understand what you make of the text of the statute. So if "relating to" modifies "law," do you deny that the law 簫簫 that the 簫簫 the Kansas law in question here relates to a controlled substance? Or do you read relating to a controlled substance to mean relating solely to controlled substances as defined by Federal law?

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: The Kansas statute in this instance relates to a Federally controlled substance sometimes. It relates to 簫簫 and that's why 簫簫

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: But the law 簫簫 the law relates to what it relates to. It doesn't relates to. It doesn't relate to something at one time and something at another time. What does the law relate to? The law relates to a long list of drugs. The great majority of them are on the Federal list. A few of them are not on the Federal list. Now, if it said relating solely to drugs on the Federal list, you'd have an argument. I just need to know what you want us to do with the text of the 簫簫 of the statute.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: When it's in the context that it's in, in 簫簫 in this sentence, again, with the focus on convicted, a 簫簫 a law that 簫簫 or I'm sorry, a word that Congress has put 簫簫 has used in our immigration statutes for decades with this particular meaning, convicted is the statutory hook, as the Court said in Moncrieffe, for the categorical approach. And it's 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: Suppose 簫簫 suppose the State's list was identical to the Federal list. You still don't know which particular. We know, but it's not part of the charge.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: So Kansas has an identical list.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: If it's identical or narrower, the person is deportable. And there are many statutes 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: And if it has one that's different, not deportable?

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: No. If 簫簫 if the 簫簫 if the substance is an element and you use the modified categorical approach, then you can determine what 簫簫 what substance is at issue and 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: Well, we don't know what substance 簫簫 in this case, we don't.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: If you don't 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: It's not charged. So it's not an element.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: That's 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: So it's not 簫簫 it's the difference that 簫簫

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: It's not in the record of conviction.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: It 簫簫 it doesn't seem to make a whole lot of sense to say deportable if the lists are identical, but if Kansas has one more on the list not deportable.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: This Court has 簫簫 well, this Court has 簫簫 has applied the categorical approach in just that way, as have many of the courts of appeals. It is a 簫簫 it is a way of determining what is the 簫簫 the 簫簫 what the person was convicted of, because you look at the facts that are 簫簫 that must be found for the conviction, and it's a mode of analysis that's been used in the immigration laws going back decades.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I mean, that's lovely, but it doesn't get you around the question that Justice Alito asked, what is the law? May I make a suggestion?

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Certainly, Justice Scalia.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Perhaps the law 簫簫 if you ask somebody what law did Mellouli violate here, it would be perfectly natural to respond by reciting one 簫簫 the elements of the law that he violated. He was convicted of possessing drug paraphernalia to store a controlled substance. And so also with any other violation of a law that names a number of controlled substances. If you ask what law did 簫簫 did you violate, he would say, I violated the 簫簫 the law that made it unlawful to have marijuana or that it made it unlawful to have cocaine. It seems to me you've got to 簫簫 you 簫簫 you have to divide the word "law" and the word "regulation" up into the component elements of the law or regulation before you can reach the result that you would like to reach.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Yes. And that's 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Is that a good suggestion?

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Yes, your Honor. And 簫簫 and in our view that's precisely the work that's done in the statute by the word "conviction" and its association with the categorical approach.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Mr. Laramore 簫簫

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: That's the framework.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: 簫簫 that is one way of looking at it, but might I suggest that you gave in a little bit too fast to Justice Alito that the phrase "relating to a controlled substance" has to modify "law." It's 簫簫 it's very true that usually in grammar a phrase like that would modify the last noun 簫簫

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: 簫簫 which in this case is "law." There is another noun in this sentence, which is "violation." And can I suggest to you that maybe, if you read this entire provision, it's pretty clearly talking about violation, because here is the way it reads. It's 簫簫 "convicted of a violation of any law relating to a controlled substance other than a marijuana offense." "Other than a marijuana offense." Now, it just doesn't make any sense to say, a law relating to a controlled substance other than a marijuana offense. What makes sense is to say, a violation of law 簫簫

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Violation.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: 簫簫 other than a marijuana offense. So this last phrase, "other than a marijuana offense," which is an exception and so you say what's it an exception to? The offense is an exception to the violation. So the violation is the key word doing the work in this phrase.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Yes. And "law" really has to be in the sentence to support "States, United States, and 簫簫 and foreign country." Other 簫簫 otherwise, that, which is an important concept in the law, couldn't be sensibly 簫簫 sensibly there.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: It certainly is possible for "relating to" to modify "violation." It's an awkward way of phrasing it, but that is possible. It's a noun. It could modify that. So what would the violation be? Is the 簫簫 the violation then is not 簫簫 the violation, I take it, then, would be the alien's conduct, what the alien did.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: I think that's right. I think that's right.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Is that right?

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: I believe so.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: So what then happens if the alien's conduct relates to a Federally controlled substance, but the conviction is under a law that does not relate to a Federally controlled substance? What if the alien, for example, is convicted of burglarizing a house for the purpose of stealing drugs that are in the house?

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: It 簫簫

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: The law doesn't relate to a controlled substance, but the conduct does relate to a controlled substance.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Right. And the deportability rises and falls on what the person is convicted of.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Yes. It's not the conduct. I think you misspoke when you said that. It's 簫簫 the violation is the crime of conviction.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: I certainly didn't mean to say conduct if I did, and I know that conviction is the key concept here and it always has been the key concept as we've applied this set of laws to 簫簫 to our immigration, to noncitizens.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: A few moments ago, I understood you to concede when Justice Ginsburg asked the question that if there is an exact parallel between the State law and the Federal law, your client would be deportable.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: So the fact that there's a 簫簫 it's a drug paraphernalia conviction has nothing to do with the 簫簫 the issue before us?

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Well, the um 簫簫 the 簫簫 the drug paraphernalia statute under which he's convicted requires that the conduct be associated with a controlled 簫簫 a Kansas controlled substance. So if the Kansas controlled substance list is the same as the Section 802 list, and then the conviction satisfies the statute.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: So, so long as the drug offense is related to, then the paraphernalia offense is related to? So it doesn't make any difference that we're dealing with paraphernalia?

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: I 簫簫 I think I understand your question, and I think that the answer to your question is yes, because the paraphernalia statute specifically incorporates the controlled substance schedule from Kansas.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: So you don't get any help by what seems to be the case, that this sock would not count as drug paraphernalia if we were 簫簫 if we were dealing with a Federal prosecution.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: You are absolutely right that it would not. Possession of paraphernalia is not a Federal offense. One cannot be prosecuted Federally for possessing drug paraphernalia.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: But the question I wanted to ask 簫簫

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: But that 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: 簫簫 is a sock considered drug paraphernalia under Federal law?

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: It would not, be because the Federal statute says that 簫簫 I don't have the language in my head, but the concept is that to be paraphernalia it 簫簫 its usual purpose has to be to facilitate drug sales or drug use or one of those things that paraphernalia does. And the Kansas definition is far broader than that. It's really any object that can be used to store or facilitate storage of or facilitate use of a controlled substance.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, that's why I would have thought your answers to me would have been different because you would want to say that the paraphernalia is an additional hurdle. Just because the drugs relate to the Federal drugs doesn't mean that you can say the paraphernalia does, because it covers all sorts of things that the Federal law doesn't.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: That's correct. It does 簫簫 it does cover many things that the Federal law doesn't. And it would 簫簫 it would be inappropriate. It 簫簫 that shows why this isn't the conduct that Congress was trying to address when it 簫簫

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: What if the 簫簫 what if someone was convicted of possession of a pipe that is very well designed to smoke crack, but maybe conceivably could be used to smoke a little bit of tobacco. So it 簫簫 maybe it's lawfully sold in some places because it could have a legitimate use. What would 簫簫 what would happen then?

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: The question is what the person is convicted of and 簫簫

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: He's convicted of possessing a crack pipe.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: If he is convicted of possessing 簫簫

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: It's necessary to show that 簫簫 that there was crack in the pipe?

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Not under the Kansas statute. The Kansas statute just says that the 簫簫 that the object has to have 簫簫 have been used or is intended to be used 簫簫

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, in your view, in order to make 簫簫 in order to make this count as a 簫簫 as a deportable offense, it would be necessary to show that there actually was crack in the pipe or that he actually used the pipe to smoke crack.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Not the way we read the statute. But the conviction would have to involve a controlled substance and would 簫簫 if it's both tobacco and crack, the crack would have to be in the 簫簫 in the conviction.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: It has to involve a controlled 簫簫 a Federally controlled substance.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: It has to involve a Federally controlled, Section 802 controlled substance, yes.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: That 簫簫 that's the linchpin.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, crack is a Federally controlled substance.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: So I didn't understand the answer to your question. Would 簫簫 would it be 簫簫 would the crack pipe itself be enough or do you have to show actual crack?

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: The crack pipe itself would be enough, as long as there was proof or a pleading to its use with, at some point, or its intention to be used with crack. That's 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Well, well, well, wait. Only 簫簫 only using the modified approach so that you would show it was 簫簫 it was intended to be used with crack. But on its face, if the Kansas statute still covers crack, yes, but a lot of other stuff 簫簫

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: 簫簫 that alone would not support the conviction, would it?

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: That is 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: You 簫簫 you'd have to go to look to see what he was actually convicted of.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Right. And 簫簫 and our analysis, again, as I point to that lengthy appendix, is that the great majority of State statutes that cover the primary drug activities 簫簫 manufacturing, sale, and possession 簫簫 are divisible statutes and would be subject to the modified categorical approach. There are also many statutes that fit within the Federal 簫簫 within the Federal 802 schedule under the categorical approach. There are State statutes that outlaw marijuana possession, that outlaw narcotic possession, in ways that 簫簫 that the statute just fits within the categorical approach. But 簫簫 but the modified categorical approach is what may apply most of the time and 簫簫 and it would 簫簫 it 簫簫 and really what we're doing here is, is I'm making this argument about the modified categorical approach to counter the government's policy argument, which is that our perspective would cut back on the number of people who could be deported for drug violations and 簫簫 and that's just not so. In fact, the Board of Immigration Appeals has been applying the law the way we say it should be applied for 簫簫 for all nonparaphernalia offenses for decades, and it always is deporting, if you look at their own statistics, 30,000, 35,000 people a year for drug offenses. So it's not 簫簫 the approach that we are advocating here, that has been used for many years, is not something that brings a halt to drug deportations. Drug deportations have been happening all along in 簫簫 under 簫簫 under the approach that we suggest. The government is really reading the law here to encompass all drug convictions, and it would be odd for that 簫簫 for that argument to be correct, given that the statute says 簫簫 makes this reference to Section 802, which is only Federally controlled substances. You can't really reconcile that with the Section 802 reference. The government's position reads that out of the statute. The last 簫簫 well, Congress has always 簫簫 well, the categorical approach has applied for decades. The 簫簫 the academic history takes it back to 1913, the first application of the doctrine, and Congress has been aware of that approach to the law, that approach to applying the law, has continued to pass statutes that use the word "convicted," which is what embodies the categorical approach. And under the 簫簫 under the application of that principle that we are advocating here, Congress has add 簫簫 has amended the statute numerous times since it was put into the form it's in now, which is 1986, in a way that add 簫簫 that signifies its ratification of the approach that we're suggesting. Congress has not only been aware of the categorical approach, but also has, since the outset of the statute, controlled the list of drugs that are deportable. And currently, the way the statute is set up is 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Nobody's disagreeing with the categorical approach. I don't know why you're wasting your argument on that. We 簫簫 we all agree with the categorical approach.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Well, let me 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: The question is categorical to what? That's the issue.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Well 簫簫 and just to differentiate our 簫簫 what we're saying from the government's position, which they also argue, is a species of the categorical approach, we're talking about the categorical approach as it has traditionally been applied, where the elements of 簫簫 of the conviction have to be compared to the generic Federal statute or to the statute of 簫簫

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: When you say "categorical approach," you mean the modified categorical approach, do you not?

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: In 簫簫 in a jurisdiction like Kansas or in any foreign jurisdiction that has a list of controlled substances that is a little bit broader than the Federal list, lists at least one or two that are broader than the Federal list, the pure categorical approach would never result in 簫簫 in removal; am I right?

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: No 簫簫

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: In a removable offense.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: That's not correct.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: How would it? You'd say 簫簫 the person was convicted of selling a drug that is categorized, that is on a list or part of a list, that would be the element of the offense.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: But um 簫簫

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: So you would have to look beyond that to see which particular drug. That's the modified approach, is it not?

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Many States, including Kansas, have some statutes that don't invoke their entire schedule. For example, a marijuana possession statute that stands alone. That would be 簫簫 that would fit under the categorical approach.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: That's not his hypothetical. His hypothetical is a statute that includes one drug which is not on the Federal list.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: I misunderstood. Then under the categorical approach that person would not be deportable. As 簫簫 as we've shown, we think that most of those statutes would be analyzable under the modified categorical approach, yes. Congress always has retained control over the substances that can lead to deportability. Under the current statute it's opened up the conduct, the kinds of crimes that are deportable, allowing those to be crimes defined by the States, defined by the United States or defined by foreign countries, as long as they relate to the Federal crimes. And that is 簫簫 that is shown by 簫簫 these substances that are on the State schedules that are not on the Federal schedules could be on the Federal schedules if the attorney general determined that they were serious enough drugs, that they were dangerous enough drugs. Some of the subjects 簫簫 of the substances on the Kansas schedule, TFMPP, which is a hallucinogen, for example, have been on the Federal schedule, but have been removed from the Federal schedule. So these indicate that they're 簫簫 they're substances of the sort that the government has not thought to be significant enough to even involve in Federal crimes 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Counsel, if we 簫簫 just to move you along, if we 簫簫 do you agree with the government that if we find the government's distinction between drug trade offenses like the paraphernalia statute and drug possession and distribution offenses unreasonable, do we remand to the board? And if not, why not? And if you want to save it in an answer on rebuttal, you can.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: Well, I think that the 簫簫 the BIA has had its chance to state its position and it has carved out 簫簫 this drug trade exception is really a drug paraphernalia exception. Nothing fits within drug trade except for drug paraphernalia. The things we ordinarily think of as drug trade, like selling and trafficking, are not part of the drug trade exception. So the BIA has had its chance to 簫簫 to construe the statute. I'd like to reserve the remainder of my time.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Ms. Kovner.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: There are two independently sufficient reasons why Petitioner's drug paraphernalia conviction permitted his removal. The first is that Federal law allows the removal of an alien convicted of violating a law relating to controlled substances, and State drug laws that cover hundreds of Federally controlled substances, in addition to some additional drugs, are laws that relate to Federally controlled substances.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: What 簫簫 what if the State law covers controlled substances entirely different from the Federal law? Maybe they figure the feds can take care of that list; we're just going to have a law with these. Does the State law relate to controlled substances?

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: We would say it does not relate to "controlled substances as defined in Section 802," which is the full statutory phrase.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I'm sorry, I don't understand that. You're doing the flip of Justice Ginsburg. You're 簫簫 so how many drugs on that list have to relate to 802 drugs for it to relate generally?

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: It 簫簫 we would concede, along the lines of Justice Breyer's hypothetical, that if you have a State law that controls 6,000 State簫controlled substances and one federally controlled 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: How about half and half?

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Wait, what was the answer? If you have it 簫簫

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: That 簫簫 that law has the kind of tenuous relationship to federally controlled drugs that the Court has said is not a relating to 簫簫 relation 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: It doesn't say more than tenuously related to. It says related to. Where are you smuggling in the "tenuously"?

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: I'm taking it from this Court's cases. The Court has said "relating to" is extremely broad, but the Court has said there is some outer limit at which even if there's a literal relating to relationship 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Well, that's how you get to drug paraphernalia, meaning, as Justice Kagan said, the drug paraphernalia comes in only because it relates to drug possession and distribution 簫簫

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: 簫簫 of a controlled substance under 801.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: We think that 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Or 802, what is it 簫簫 whatever it is.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: That's right. We think that crimes involving drug paraphernalia are crimes that relate to controlled substances, and there are two 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: You keep leaving out the 802. There are other sections of the criminal act that specifically say, any conviction and 簫簫 and it could be of any law, right? But this one doesn't say it that way.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: We think that, as Your Honor correctly notes, these are laws that relate to controlled substances as defined in Section 802. And there are two reasons why: The first is that a statute that 簫簫 that relates 簫簫 a statute that forbids possession of paraphernalia that's connected to hundreds of federally controlled drugs, and here nine additional substances, relates to federally controlled drugs. And the second is that 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And 簫簫 difficult 簫簫 now answer.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: How many of the laws 簫簫 how many of the controlled substances have to be on the State list? Is 50/50 enough, 75/25, 6 out of 簫簫

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: We think that 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I mean, what 簫簫 what's the 簫簫 how many have to be on the list?

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: We think that all the State laws that currently exist are laws that relate to federally controlled substances. And all of those 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But that's not true of foreign laws and this relates to foreign laws. I mean, there are some foreign countries that list many of the controlled substances the U.S. does but adds alcohol. Now, some people may think alcohol is a controlled substance, but it's not on the 802 list.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: Your Honor, we acknowledge there might be a case involving a foreign law or involving if in the future a State decided to take a very different approach, where you would have a literal relating簫to relationship, but there would be a question of whether the law was so far beyond what existed at the time and what Congress could have imagined that it no longer relates to. But Petitioner's reading of "relating to" isn't a permissible reading of the 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: But what second 簫簫 second 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Do you, please 簫簫 do you think that if we apply a modified categorical approach and focus in on the conviction here which was for a sock? Do you think a sock is more than tenuously related to 簫簫 to these Federal drugs?

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. And here's why: These drug paraphernalia statutes modified on the model act, like Kansas's, are quite broad, and we acknowledge.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Right. That's why I'm proposing modified categorical approach. He was convicted of having a sock and you think that's more than tenuously related to these Federal drugs.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: We do, because he wasn't exactly convicted of having a sock. He was convicted using an innocent item as a tool for the storage of drugs, and that's true of every drug paraphernalia conviction. Typically drug paraphernalia involves objects that have an innocent use and objects that have an illicit use. But essentially every State has decided that in order to impede the drug trade and drug use you need to prohibit those.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: What about, you said second? You said two reasons. You got your first out and I'd like to hear your second.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: You said the second, didn't you?

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: There is an additional reason, Your Honor.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: She said it.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: She didn't.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: The BIA has reasoned 簫簫 the BIA has reasoned, in a conclusion that entitled to Chevron deference, that paraphernalia statutes are statutes that attack the drug trade in general.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Okay. Now I got the second one. Now I do have one question, and it's right related to Justice Scalia's, and it seems to me that 簫簫 what he said, if I understand it correctly, makes this not too difficult a case, but you won't like it: That 簫簫 that we have long 簫簫 yes, it modifies law, but we have long, if we go back to the seminal case, Taylor. There is a Massachusetts statute. It forbids a burglary of a boat, a car, and a house. So what Justice Blackmun said 簫簫 I think it was he who wrote it 簫簫 go back to the documents to see whether he was charged with burglary of a house, a boat, or a car. So we have a tradition of looking back to the charging documents and maybe a couple of others to see what part of the statute. Now, this statute is a statute that first penalizes many, many things, packaging 簫簫 packaging is relevant here. What kind of packaging? Packaging of drugs. Okay. What drugs? It doesn't say drugs. It says controlled substance, so you have to cross簫reference. Then we look to cross簫reference, and it says controlled substance means a substance included on State schedule 12345 or part B, so it incorporates that. So then we go look to those documents, and those documents will have a long list. And we look to see here which of the documents on the list which is incorporated into the statute is he charged of packaging. Very simple. Happens it's not that simple to do, but that is absolutely traditional given our holdings on how to apply the categorical 簫簫 modified categorical system. So why not just do that right here? Solves every problem that's raised.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: Let me give two responses. One is a textual one, and the second, I think, goes to your practical point because I really think it's important that it won't actually solve the practical problems here. As to the textual point, when Your Honor talks about Taylor, I think it's important to note how different the statute here is, which is unlike many provisions that talk about being deportable or suffering some other consequence for being convicted of a crime relating to something, here it's talking about being convicted of violating a law relating to something. But I really want to address this practical point, because, Your Honor, we think the result under Petitioner's view is going to be a real patchwork in terms of what conduct is removable depending on what State you're in. And the reason why, Justice Breyer, is that it's not going to be possible to apply the modified categorical approach to many drug statutes. It will sometimes and it won't other times, and let me explain why. For instance, here, under this statute, it appears and under paraphernalia status generally, there will typically not be a requirement of a finding or an admission of what particular drug you intended to use the 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But if I understand the way the law works, right, Ms. Kovner, that's 簫簫 that is true of paraphernalia drugs, that they're indivisible with respect to the controlled substance. It's not true with respect to most possession statutes or distribution statutes or so forth. And the reason that there is that distinction is because paraphernalia offenses are generally extremely minor offenses; they're not felonies. They're just, as in this case, they're misdemeanors that are meant to catch leftover things that, you know, prosecutors plead to when they don't really want to charge a more serious offense. And so it makes good sense that all of these paraphernalia offenses would be counted out by this approach. You're counting out a bunch of trivial 簫簫 I mean, you know, "trivial," I don't want to say trivial 簫簫 minor offenses.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: Your Honor, I think two points about that. The first is that we don't think it's correct that this is going to be just paraphernalia offenses and not any possession or distribution offenses. And if you look at the cases that are cited on page 31 of our brief, they illustrate that, which is the conviction records are not necessarily specifying the drug that's involved in a particular offense. And so you're going to have a really haphazard system.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Well, right now even your brief admits that the BIA is applying a different standard to possession and distribution offenses. It's applying the 802 specification.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: Your 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And it's trying to draw a difference. I don't 簫簫 I still don't understand how it does it, but my point is that you're proving it's 簫簫 you're living by the 802 distinction with distribution and possession.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: Your Honor, I'm not sure that that's true. We think that the BIA's precedent at this point in unclear. The BIA 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Yeah, what you're hoping for is that they will change that one, too. But let's answer what's happened up until now.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: Yes. As to what's happened up until now, I don't think that's clear. My understanding is that the BIA will generally try to seek removal of people who have been convicted 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Because they have discretion. If they're not automatically removable and they look at the underlying papers and decide it's a more serious crime than 簫簫 than the 簫簫 than the strict removability statute, they let the person 簫簫 they remove this person anyway.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: I don't think that's correct, Your Honor. What we're seeking here is the authority to seek removal for persons who've committed these offenses. If 簫簫 in this case, for instance, if we were to not prevail, we would lack the authority, the government would lack the authority to remove Petitioner no matter how serious it regarded his underlying conduct.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: A couple of sentences ago you said that a sock, when it's used in connection with the drug activity, is sufficiently related to the Federal Controlled Substances Law. What about a gun? Those are often used in connection with drug activity. So is a law concerning gun possession or gun control or whatever, a State law that relates to the Federal Controlled Substances Law?

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: Your Honor, I think this would be a 簫簫 a difficult question on either of our sides, which is to say, for 簫簫 for both parties there would be some set of statutes that are related to 簫簫 that have some connection to drugs indirectly and there would be a question of whether those statutes count. We don't think that a statute that addresses guns merely because guns and drugs have some, you know, relationship 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, yes. But if a statute addresses socks, it does.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: Well, here, Your Honor, it's not a statute addressing socks. It's a statute 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: It was addressing socks when used in connection with drug activity. I'm talking about guns when used in connection with drug activity.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: Oh. Your Honor, a statute like the 924(c) that applies to guns when used in connection with drug activity, we think would be a law that relates to controlled substances. We think statutes that regulate conduct, that um leads to the proliferation of drugs or the use of drugs is a poor statute relating to the drug trade.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Under your definition 簫簫 under your understanding of this, what practical work is done by the phrase as defined in Section 802 of Title XXI?

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: Your Honor 簫簫

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: You said a couple of minutes ago, and I had assumed this to be true, that under the laws of all the States, the great majority of controlled substances are on the Federal list, and there 簫簫 in some instances 簫簫 are a few additional ones and you said that all of those laws would be covered. If that's the case, then I don't see what was achieved by putting the reference to the Federal list in the statute. They might have just said relating to a controlled substance.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: Your Honor, we think that this parenthetical clarifies that 簫簫 the meaning of controlled substance, the thing that it has to relate to are the substances as defined in Federal law. There is another colloquial meaning of controlled substance if you look in, like, Black's Law Dictionary, which is just any regulated drug and that would apply to, for instance, prescription drugs that aren't controlled under the Controlled Substances Act.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Would it have to be drugs? I mean, there are some States that ban pate, for example, right?

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: You wouldn't want that swept into this statute, would you?

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: That would not be a statute under the law of a State relating to a controlled substance as defined in Section 802, because it wouldn't be regulated under a State's direct statutes that also include the Section 802 substances. And if I could just explain why we think that Congress enacted or 簫簫 a law that applies to any person who violates a State law relating to controlled substances. When Congress enacted the 簫簫 the provision of the INA that's currently in effect, it acted against the backdrop of State laws that were designed to tightly coordinate with Federal laws and that were designed to produce the kind of regime that they have, in fact, produced for any 簫簫 any overlap between the State and Federal schedules is the overwhelming proportion of the drugs that are controlled on State schedules. And, Justice Breyer, if the Court were to hold that those laws aren't laws relating to controlled substances, whether an alien is removable under those State laws designed to parallel with Federal laws is going to depend on what 簫簫 what kinds of findings the State requires to be made in connection with those possession 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Well, that's 簫簫 it wouldn't make that law a nullity. Indeed, the person would be deportable provided when you went through the State charge, the State charge was you violated the packaging for use statute, packaging a controlled substance, in respect to Item 13 under Schedule II, which they could say, anyway, and that happens to be heroin. And if, in fact, when you went through Schedule III, Part 4(a), the word there was "alcohol," then it wouldn't. Now, that's simple. And if the State's charging practice is such that they don't tell you, that's equivalent to a State charging practice that in Massachusetts didn't tell you whether you were accused of burglary of a boat, of a house or of a car. And if the State wants to do that, I guess it might be up to them if you get a guilty plea and then you could not deport the person.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: But Congress didn't want that result, Justice Breyer. Congress 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Well, I want 簫簫 a lot of people 簫簫 but they can't control the State charging practices and the States are likely, for reasons of information, to put in the charge the whole thing, including the correct reference on the schedule. So is that the only thing you find wrong with it? And I agree with you that that is a slight problem, that 簫簫 that we'll read back the conviction of four years ago in Missouri and we will see that it doesn't tell us what item on the schedule. That's the problem that you see.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: That 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Any other?

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: Your Honor, that's a significant practical problem that's going to introduce widespread variation in the deportability of people who have committed crimes involving Federally controlled substances.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But 簫簫 but as Ms. 簫簫 as Justice Sotomayor suggested, it's a significant practical problem that's implicated in the current practice of the BIA.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: I 簫簫 I'm 簫簫 I don't think that's correct. We think the current BIA precedent is quite ambiguous on the possession point and we think that 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: It at least might be implicated in the current practice of the BIA.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: We think that the BIA's precedent on this point is unclear. But, Justice Breyer, our argument isn't just about the practical consequences. It's also about the best reading of this text. We don't think this text is 簫簫 is ambiguous. It talks about violations of laws relating to 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: You have to choose. As you well know, the problem you just mentioned is a problem that we're just having a terrible time with in respect to the violence add簫ons. And it's 簫簫 stems from the fact that State laws differ one from the other enormously. So we either have that problem or we have the problem of you have a general statute and which does it apply more to and so forth. Or you have the problem of do you look 簫簫 as I think Justice Kagan's approach 簫簫 do you 簫簫 you look at the individual facts of the case and require them to prove it out again. I 簫簫 I don't see how we escape one serious problem or another.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: I agree. And let me propose that there are 簫簫 there's difficulties under either approach 簫簫 approach. Let me tell you why I think this is the best approach here. The first is I think the text signal that Congress chose our approach here. And the text is different from any other similar provisions. The second is that there's 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: It's 簫簫 it's exactly like it. When Congress intended to say 簫簫 and I can point to at least three provisions 簫簫 when it wanted to say any controlled substance without reference to 簫簫 to 802, it said, any controlled substance. And when it wanted to limit it to 802, it defined the controlled substance as being listed in 802.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: Your Honor 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So it knows how to differentiate the two.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: The 簫簫 the textual contrast that we find most revealing here is that there are provisions of the INA that talk about being removable for a crime related to controlled substances, and then you would do the categorical approach just as you're proposing. But let me just mention the practical reason why we think our approach is preferable, Justice Breyer, and why we think that Congress chose it, which is that Congress acted against the backdrop of State drug laws like Kansas's, which 簫簫 which weren't States just enacting random lists of substances as their controlled substances. These laws are designed, as 簫簫 as Congress put it, as an interlocking trellis to closely relate to Federal law. So Congress wanted 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But, Ms. Kovner, I would think that the history works against you in the following way: Because the prior statute here was a statute that listed all these Federally controlled substances. And that eventually got to be impractical, there were too many of them, they kept changing and so Congress went to this approach. But it was clear in the old statute that the only way that you could be held deportable was if you had been convicted of an offense, you know, possession or distribution or whatever, with a Federally controlled substance. And there's no reason to think that in enacting this statute that 簫簫 that Congress meant to do something broader than that. There's every reason to think it wanted to do the exact same thing. If you're convicted of a controlled substance 簫簫 of possession of a controlled substance, you are deportable. If you're not, if it's only Sudafed, you're not.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: Your Honor, I don't think that's correct. Congress, in the prior versions of the statute, did specify particular acts that your conduct had to involve and also specified a narrower list of substances that your conduct had to relate to. But I don't think it's clear that Congress believed 簫簫 there's nothing in the legislative history or other documents to suggest that Congress believed that "relating to" language meant involving 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: No. But the question is, when Congress makes a change like that, which we know is a change for a particular reason because the list of substances was getting out of control 簫簫 it's very much like yesterday's case; I don't know if you're familiar with that 簫簫 but Congress makes a change for one reason, suggests 簫簫 does not make any 簫簫 any suggestion that this change is supposed to do something else and something much bigger, which is extend this deportability provision to controlled substances that aren't part of the Federal list, and there's just no reason to think that Congress did that in making this change.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: Your Honor, the 簫簫 the "relating to" language, which is very broad language, is within the prior statute, and it's within the statute now. We don't think there's an indication that Congress read that statute narrowly in the prior versions. We think the plain meaning of that 簫簫 that language is quite broad. Your Honor, let me just mention. There is a second reason that we've talked principally about the first one, but the BIA was also entitled to Chevron deference for its separate reasoning pertaining to paraphernalia. The BIA has for many, many years reasoned that there are some crimes that are related to the drug trade in general, not necessarily to any individual substance. And the earliest case on this was a case about 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Espinoza came out when?

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: The case I'm thinking of is Martinez簫Gomez from 1972, which talks about opening a drug 簫簫 essentially, opening a drug den, and they say that's a crime that doesn't necessarily relate just to any individual substance; it bears a relationship to all substances. And then in Martinez Espinoza, more recently, um the Court 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: That was a Federal crime, though. It had to relate to a controlled substance on the Federal list, to all drugs on the Federal list.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: Your Honor, the reasoning of Martinez簫Gomez and then the reasoning of Martinez Espinoza is that these are crimes that don't necessarily have to bear a relationship to a single substance. For instance, drug paraphernalia are tools that can be used with multiple substances. So you might possess, you know, scales and baggies for the purpose of packaging a particular drug on one occasion, but those items can just as easily facilitate those activities with respect to 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: But a 簫簫 a sock. I mean, one of the strange things about this case is you have a disjunction. A sock doesn't count as drug paraphernalia under the Federal law, but it does under the State law; it means any container. And then to take that State law 簫簫 I mean, suppose we 簫簫 we have a conviction for one of those drugs that's not on the Federal list, just for possession of that drug, and then we have another conviction where that drug is inside a sock and it's for drug paraphernalia. So in the one case you would say not removable, if it's just the substance itself, and in the other case, because of the sock, it becomes removable?

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: Your Honor, I agree that I think a strange feature of this case is that it involves an item that's not usually thought of as drug paraphernalia. I think the classic paraphernalia items and the ones you see most often prosecuted in Kansas and in other States are things like hyperdermic needles and scales and substances that are used to mix with drugs before sale, and I think it's easiest to see in that case why the BIA's reasoning makes sense, which is, those items, when they're possessed by somebody in connection, even with a State簫controlled drug, are tools 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I don't think that's the important point that Justice Ginsburg's making. She's saying under Federal law, this person can't be convicted for that drug 簫簫 that's not a drug on the Federal list 簫簫 but it can 簫簫 or the State list 簫簫 but can be convicted, under your theory, for possessing the sock and that non簫illicit drug in the sock.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: For possessing the sock. And I think it's easiest 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: With the 簫簫 with that particular unscheduled item.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: And I think it's easiest to see why the BIA drew this conclusion when you talk about the more classical tools of paraphernalia, which is, if somebody possesses scales and distribution baggies and cut in connection with a State簫controlled substance 簫簫 say it's synthetic marijuana, an item that's not Federally controlled at the time 簫簫 those are tools that could be used in connection with a Federal drug as well. And so this 簫簫 the BIA reasoned that there are certain crimes that 簫簫 that involve conduct that could facilitate the distribution of one drug just as easily as another.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Do we 簫簫 do we even know that this is a 簫簫 a proper conviction under Kansas law, that the Kansas statute actually means what it's been interpreted here to mean? The Petitioner pled guilty to this, I think, because he got a break, because he could have been charged with something 簫簫 if, in fact, he had Adderall, which is a Federally controlled substance 簫簫 he could have been charged with something more serious. So he pled to this misdemeanor. But it's really hard to believe that the Kansas statute actually regards as drug paraphernalia anything that is used at any time to contain a controlled substance. Suppose somebody buys marijuana or some other drug and it's in a plastic bag. So the plastic bag is 簫簫 that's one violation. The person puts it in a pocket; that's another violation. The pocket is 簫簫 is drug paraphernalia. Takes it out of the pocket and puts it in the glove compartment of a car; that's a third violation. The car is equipment that's used to store it. Takes it out of that, puts it in something 簫簫 it 簫簫 it can't really mean this, but we don't have State court interpretations.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: That's right. And if I could just make two brief points about that. You're right that this was a result of a plea, and as a result, we don't know exactly what Kansas courts would say about this application, which is a very unusual application. But I think that illustrates, Justice Alito, I think, the bad consequences of Petitioner's view, which is, for Petitioner, even if it was cocaine that was stored in the sock, if he pleads guilty to the paraphernalia offense, the BIA will lack any ability to take enforcement action against him, because he's convicted of a paraphernalia crime, and the nature of the substance doesn't 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: If he had cocaine in his sock, he would probably be convicted of possession of cocaine.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: But 簫簫

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: He was convicted of paraphernalia here because he had four pills of Adderall, which if you go to half the colleges in America, people 簫簫 you know, and just randomly pick somebody, there would be a decent chance 簫簫

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: I think 簫簫 I think Justice Alito's explanation of this case explains why that's not so. In a plea bargain, prosecutors may be willing to let somebody plead to a drug paraphernalia offense in lieu of a possession offense, and Congress did not want the fact that a State or local prosecutor allows that kind of plea bargain to mean that immigration authorities lack the ability to remove somebody convicted of a crime that relates to a controlled substance.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, I would have thought the opposite inference. If it's not such a big deal that the State is willing to let him cop a plea to drug paraphernalia, why should that be the basis for deportation under Federal law?

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: Your Honor, we don't think that Congress viewed drug crimes that way. The way it wrote this removal provision was to say that any crime relating to controlled substances permits the BIA to seek removal. And that's because, we think, Congress saw drug crimes as quite serious. This Court has recognized that, and Congress has essentially said so in other statutes. So we 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, but, I mean, the only 簫簫 it's because you give "relating to" such a broad construction that you get the 簫簫 what I think is the unusual situation that I talked about, which is that the State thinks it's a very minor offense and yet it can become so significant that the person's deported.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: But Petitioner's approach is a poor way of reading a crime severity distinction into the statute. We don't think that's what the language allows, for minor drug crimes to be set aside as a basis for 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Why is it a poor way? I mean, the drugs that the Federal government believes are the most serious are listed in 802. And so if you have to prove, under the modified categorical approach, that one of those drugs was the intended use, what is so horrible and 簫簫 and dissonant about affecting the intent based on the drugs Congress has chosen to list?

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: Your Honor, it's a bad way because serious drug crimes involving Federally controlled substances will not be removable under Petitioner's view. And to get 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: No, no, no, no, no. We 簫簫 I know that you keep saying "for many years," but it's not that many years. And the BIA has routinely required in distribution and possession cases that you identify the drug.

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: Your 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And so they haven't found it impossible in thousands of those cases. It may be more difficult in the drug paraphernalia case because Justice Kagan was right. They're minor offenses; often, States won't bother with pleas that are very lengthy or very complex. But that's a happenstance. That's not 簫簫

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: It would 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Does it call for changing the natural reading of the sentence?

turns.name: KOVNER
turns.text: No. We 簫簫 Your Honor, we're seeking the application of what we think is the best reading of the sentence. Your Honor, I think if you look at the BIA cases that we cite on Page 31, they show that Petitioner's approach will prevent the removal of aliens committing crimes involving Federally controlled substances in many cases because the conviction records aren't going to specify the drug involved. Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Laramore, you have four minutes left.

turns.name: LARAMORE
turns.text: First, the BIA's position is not unclear or in flux. The BIA reaffirmed in a case called Ferreira last year, cited on page 22 of our reply brief, its position, just as Justice Sotomayor said, that the substance has to be identified in um possession and sale cases and it has to be an 802 substance. That's a system that has been in place for decades. It has worked. It has allowed the deportation of drug criminals. It doesn't need to be changed. I'd ask the Court to focus on 簫簫 on something that 簫簫 that Ms. Kovner raised, which is what Congress intended and knew about when it enacted this version of the statute in 1996, and 簫簫 I'm sorry, 1986. And she said that they knew about the Uniform Controlled Substances Act and that may have had an influence, although there's nothing that shows in the legislative history that it did. But Congress also knew about the categorical approach when it put the word "convicted" into the statute at that time. And it knew that the categorical approach and the modified categorical approach would be used when it 簫簫 when it invoked the word "convicted." And that's what we're asking the Court to reaffirm now. The word "law" that we've talked about so much that's in the sentence, or "law and regulation," is necessary in the statute to support what 簫簫 another thing that Congress tried to do in that law, in that statute, which was to clearly invoke States' authority and foreign countries' authority to convict 簫簫 to allow those convictions to support deportation. And if 簫簫 there's 簫簫 it was a convenient way to insert that concept into the statute. I would also point out that the underpinnings of the Martinez Espinoza case, which is the one that they rely on now 簫簫 this is the so簫called drug trade exception, this 簫簫 which is really just a drug paraphernalia exception 簫簫 are not nearly as strong as the government suggests. Martinez簫Gomez is a case about 簫簫 I'm not quite sure what you call it 簫簫 a drug house, a place where drugs are commonly used. But there was no indication in Martinez簫Gomez that it necessarily involved any substances that were not Section 802 substances. It's not very strong support for this creation out of really whole cloth a drug trade exception. Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
